summary: In this introductory text and the essay 'Of Essay Writing', David Hume outlines his project to bridge the gap between the 'learned' and 'conversable' worlds. He argues that philosophy must be grounded in the experiences of common life and social interaction, warning against reclusive academic study. The text also summarizes his views on aesthetics, political obligation, and the positive role of luxury in societal refinement.

principles.statement: The separation of the learned and conversable worlds is detrimental to both.
principles.rationale: Without learning, conversation devolves into idle gossip; without conversation and common life, learning becomes barbarous, chimerical, and disconnected from reality.
principles.source_quote: The separation of the learned from the conversable world seems to have been the great defect of the last age, and must have had a very bad influence both on books and company

principles.statement: Philosophy and learning must be grounded in the experience of common life.
principles.rationale: Reclusive study leads to unintelligible and chimerical conclusions because it fails to consult the real-world experience found in society.
principles.source_quote: ...what could be expected from men who never consulted experience in any of their reasonings, or who never searched for that experience, where alone it is to be found, in common life and conversation?

principles.statement: Political obligation is based on societal necessity, not an original democratic contract.
principles.rationale: Historically, almost all governments were founded on usurpation or conquest; the true bond that obligates citizens to a government is the practical interest and necessities of society.
principles.source_quote: ...the general obligation, which binds us to government, is the interest and necessities of society

principles.statement: Correct moral and aesthetic judgement requires adopting a steady, general point of view.
principles.rationale: Momentary distractions, prejudices, and individual proximity distort judgements. A general viewpoint overcomes these subjective limitations to reveal universal principles.
principles.source_quote: ...we fix on some steady and general points of view.

mental_models.name: The Commerce of Knowledge
mental_models.description: Viewing the relationship between academia ('the learned') and society ('the conversable') as two distinct nations that require diplomatic and commercial exchange. The raw materials of thought come from common life, while the manufacturing and structuring of those thoughts is done by learning.
mental_models.example: Hume considers himself a 'resident or ambassador from the dominions of learning to those of conversation,' importing and exporting ideas between them to maintain a balance of trade.

heuristics: To correct moral and aesthetic misjudgements, fix on a steady and general point of view, overlooking your present situation and prejudices.

signature_quotes.text: I cannot but consider myself as a kind of resident or ambassador from the dominions of learning to those of conversation...
signature_quotes.context: Hume describing his goal as an essayist to bridge the gap between academic philosophy and polite society.

signature_quotes.text: The materials of this commerce must chiefly be furnished by conversation and common life: the manufacturing of them alone belongs to learning.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the symbiotic relationship between everyday experience and academic refinement.

signature_quotes.text: The separation of the learned from the conversable world seems to have been the great defect of the last age...
signature_quotes.context: Critiquing the isolation of scholars from everyday society.

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Shutting learning up in colleges and cells, secluded from the world, makes belles lettres barbarous and philosophy chimerical because it ignores the experience found only in common life.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on the 'original contract' theory of government.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It ignores historical reality (that most governments were founded on usurpation or conquest) and fails to recognize that the true foundation of government is societal necessity.
